title: ‘The window is now’: Microsoft pitches solution to AI grid crisis
full_text:
Technology giant Microsoft has called for the creation of an Australian government energy fund to handle the AI boom, and warned a major change in the power grid is needed amid rising political and community concerns over electricity demand.
The tech operator will spend $25bn in Australia – its biggest investment in the country – in the next three years, on top of $5bn building nine new data centres in Australia as part of a massive expansion of its AI supercomputing and cloud infrastructure.
The AI and data centre investment boom has triggered a surge in electricity demand, and Microsoft said the creation of a new “AI in Energy Fund” was needed to transform Australia’s ageing power grid, mimicking existing funds in California and the UK that had de-risked investment.
A body with a similar remit to the Australian Renewable Energy Agency, which helps fund projects to accelerate green investment, could transform the ability of the sprawling national electricity market to capitalise on the tech revolution with a focus on AI and grid optimisation.
“Underinvestment in AI, particularly for transformative applications, is being driven by inertia among energy players,” Microsoft said in an Australian report this week.
Regulatory bodies have failed to provide clear frameworks and guidance on the role of AI in the energy sector, while industry is grappling with ageing assets and legacy technology that is hobbling a move to modern technology, according to the company.
Microsoft has called for a market study on AI’s current role, deployment levels and opportunities to scale, plus a clear national policy position on AI in the electricity system and adapted regulatory settings around data sharing and regulated funding models to support uptake.
The tech titan’s intervention lands at a critical time as federal and state governments engage in a scrap for investment dollars to prop up dismal economic growth.
Australia’s data-centre investment pipeline tops $155bn and the total number of projects will rival the size of the mining investment boom, according to Westpac.
However, the industry is also facing growing opposition from communities who are worried about noise, pollution and higher energy costs from the artificial-intelligence factories.
In the US, the AI boom has pushed the nation’s largest power-grid operator to the brink of a supply crisis. Tech companies have railed against proposed rules that would require data centres to build their own power sources or go dark during demand surges.
Assistant Science and Technology Minister Andrew Charlton said he was a believer that data centres could boost the power grid while cautioning that any developments must pay their own way, not boost household energy bills, and develop extra sources of electricity supplies.
“We certainly think that if we get it right data centres can contribute to Australia’s energy grid,” Dr Charlton told The Australian, adding he would consider the Microsoft proposal.
“We’re working hard with the different data centre providers to talk about how that might occur in a way that doesn’t put upwards pressure on prices and improves grid stability.”
Microsoft has urged quick action as the race among nations to win investment heats up, and acknowledged the investment boom had triggered questions and issues among political leaders and local communities.
“The window is now. Electricity demand is accelerating, putting pressure on existing infrastructure, and community and political concerns are increasing, with fear that the cost is being passed to consumers,” a Mandala research paper commissioned by Microsoft said.
“Making the case for the role of AI and demonstrating its capabilities to drive improvements in the affordability and reliability of the electricity system will be crucial in growing social licence for further build-out of data centres.”
Industry Minister Tim Ayres first laid out expectations for data centres in March, with operators ordered to find new renewable energy supplies and pay grid expenses so costs are not passed on to consumers or businesses. Queensland was the sole outlier that failed to approve the plan at a meeting of energy ministers in May after raising questions over costs, benefits, and risks.
Dr Charlton said he was confident an upcoming meeting of energy ministers this month would lead to a united position on the renewables plan.
“The essence of this is to make sure that data centres don’t increase power prices for people,” Dr Charlton said.
Major electricity transmission companies including Transgrid, Ausnet and Vicgrid have already warned the rush to install major data centres has hiked security risks to Australia’s power grid, as authorities scramble to limit the threat of blackouts.
Experts are concerned that dozens of data centres face being knocked out simultaneously, as a wave of new power generation tests the technical limits of the electricity system, and jacks up the risk of supply shortages or even blackouts.
The national rule-maker has proposed new technical standards that would require large data centres to stay connected during disturbances on the electricity grid and respond to instability, reflecting concerns the facilities behave more like power generators than traditional energy users.
Microsoft’s Australian investment is focused on deploying advanced AI processors and enhancing local Azure supercomputing capacity to capitalise on the agentic or autonomous AI era.
Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella said a vast spending spree is necessary to catalyse an economic shift, particularly in markets like Australia, where productivity has been stagnant.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
